meta.title: Unit 5 — AI and Ethical Use of AI
meta.subtitle: Computer Information · Law students
meta.author: Academic Content Factory

slides.title: Unit 5
slides.subtitle: AI and Ethical Use of AI
slides.notes: Teach Unit 5 as a modular block (6 hours).

slides.title: Unit 5 — AI and Ethical Use in Law
slides.notes: Six hours, 10% weight. Pair every tool demo with ethics critique. Never paste confidential client data into public AI tools.

slides.title: AI Tools for Legal Research
bullets: Database AI: natural-language search in case law platforms
Summarization tools condense judgments and statutes
Citation verification still requires primary sources
Compare AI summary against original judgment text
Institution may provide approved research assistants
Open web chatbots are not substitutes for databases
slides.notes: Demo research workflow with verification step. Stress that subscription databases remain authoritative; AI assists retrieval and summary.

slides.title: Generative AI Basics
bullets: Creates new text, images, or code from learned patterns
Large language models predict likely next words
Trained on vast corpora — not live legal databases
Useful for outlines, rephrasing, and brainstorming
Outputs may be wrong, outdated, or jurisdictionally off
Always label AI-assisted work per college policy
slides.notes: Define generative AI for exams. Contrast with traditional search. Students should explain why training data limits legal reliability.

slides.title: AI in Judiciary and Legal Analytics
bullets: Analytics dashboards track case pendency and outcomes
NLP tools assist classification and document clustering
Some jurisdictions pilot AI transcription and translation
Predictive analytics raise fairness and transparency issues
Lawyers must understand tool limits in court contexts
Regulatory frameworks evolving globally
slides.notes: Survey level only. Link to professional responsibility—analytics can reflect historical bias in court data.

slides.title: AI-Assisted Drafting
bullets: Tools suggest clauses, rephrase paragraphs, check tone
Useful starting point for notices and memo outlines
Lawyer must verify facts, law, and citations
Comparative lab: human draft vs AI-assisted draft
Never file AI output without full professional review
Client confidentiality rules apply to all inputs
slides.notes: Practical 8 alignment: human vs AI drafting comparison. AI accelerates first drafts; accountability stays with the advocate.

slides.title: Privacy and Data Protection
bullets: Client names, facts, and documents are confidential
Public AI tools may store or train on your inputs
Use anonymized prompts or approved enterprise tools
Data protection laws govern personal information handling
Breach of confidentiality is a professional conduct issue
When in doubt, do not paste matter details into AI
slides.notes: Core ethics topic. Scenario exams: student pastes client facts into ChatGPT—identify the confidentiality breach and correct approach.

slides.title: Bias in AI Systems
bullets: AI reflects biases in training data and design choices
May disadvantage groups in risk scoring or search ranking
Historical court data can encode systemic inequities
Lawyers must question AI recommendations critically
Document limitations when relying on analytics
Fairness and access to justice are legal values at stake
slides.notes: Define bias for exams: systematic unfairness in outputs. Give examples: skewed training data affecting predictions or summaries.

slides.title: Ethics Caution — Professional Responsibility
bullets: You remain accountable for every filed document
AI does not hold a law license or appear in court
Disclose AI assistance when policy or court requires
Never cite AI-generated cases without verification
Competence includes understanding tools you use
Ethical practice means verify, protect, and disclose
slides.notes: Ethics caution slide — pause for discussion. Use a vignette: fake citation discovered before filing. What should the student do?

slides.title: Limitations of AI in Law
bullets: Hallucination — confident but fabricated citations or facts
Jurisdiction errors — foreign law presented as local
No real-world awareness of your specific matter
Cannot exercise ethical judgment or courtroom advocacy
Training cut-off dates miss recent amendments
AI assists research and drafting; it does not practice law
slides.notes: Limitations are exam favorites alongside ethics. Hallucination and jurisdiction mismatch are concrete examples students must articulate.

slides.title: Human-in-the-Loop Practice
bullets: Human lawyer reviews every AI output before reliance
Verification loop: generate → check → correct → approve
Senior supervision for student AI-assisted work
Maintain research logs showing primary sources checked
Reject and rewrite when AI output fails quality bar
Professional standard: accountable human judgment last
slides.notes: Human-in-the-loop is the operational answer to AI risk. Define it clearly—accountable human review over all consequential outputs.

slides.title: Responsible and Ethical AI Use
bullets: Use approved tools; know their terms of service
Minimize personal data in prompts; anonymize facts
Cross-check all legal propositions against primary sources
Educate clients about AI limits when relevant
Stay updated on bar council and college AI policies
Ethics is not optional — it is part of legal competence
slides.notes: Synthesize Unit 5 into a responsible-use checklist. Connect to Course Outcome 8: Evaluate AI for ethics and professional limits.

slides.title: Unit 5 — Exam Tips
bullets: Short notes: bias, privacy, hallucination, human-in-the-loop
Scenario: identify confidentiality breach in AI use
Explain why AI citations must be verified independently
List three limitations of AI in legal practice
Compare human drafting vs AI-assisted drafting duties
Pitfall: assuming AI practices law or is always current
slides.notes: Ethics vignettes dominate Unit 5 exams. Practice writing short structured answers: issue, rule/policy, application, conclusion.

slides.title: Unit 5 — Next steps
bullets: Complete unit labs
Attempt unit Q&A bank
Submit unit assignment
Revise using revision sheet
slides.notes: Point students to materials/labs and materials/qa for this unit.
